the information technology act, 2000 amended the definition of bankers' books in the bankers' books evidence act, 1891. the following changes were made by this act: amendment in section 2(3): earlier the definition of bankers' books only contained ledgers, daybooks, cash books, accounts books as well as other books used in the ordinary course of business in a bank. after the amendment, it includes records stored in microfilm, magnetic tape or any other form of mechanical or electronic data retrieval mechanism. section 2(8)(b): this sub-clause was added to the definition of a certified copy which includes a printout of any entry that is stored in microfilm, magnetic tape or any other form of mechanical or electronic data retrieval mechanism that itself ensures the accuracy of such printout as a copy of the entry section 2a: this section was added to deal with certification requirements for admissibility of a certified copy in the printout form. it provides which certificates shall be required by the person in charge of the computer system. om prakash v. central bureau of investigation: in this case, it was held that section 65b of the indian evidence act is pari materia to section 2a of the bankers' books evidence act. therefore they should be construed together. moreover in anvar p.v v. p.k. basheer and ors, it was observed that a special law will always prevail over general law. this implies that even though there is a provision (section 65b) for electronic records under the indian evidence act, the provision that deals specifically with the admissibility of banking records in electronic form are section 2a of the bankers' books evidence act. thus, following the principle of 'generalia specialibus' section 2a will be preferred over section 65 b with respect to dealing with banking records in electronic form. sonu @ amar v. state of haryana: in this case, it was observed that the test to determine an objection pertaining to the admissibility of banking records should be allowed or not depends on whether or not the defect in question could be cured at the stage of marking the document and the party tendering evidence could have resorted to the regular mode of proof. radheshyam g. garg v. safiya bai ibrahim lightwalla: in this case, it was observed that when an agent of bank signs a certificate validating the record to be a true copy of the original, maintained in the usual course of business and kept in the bank's custody then the court should not focus on all the conditions provided under section 2(8) of the act and take a a witness in the context of electronic evidence and the it act plays a crucial role in verifying the authenticity and integrity of electronic records. the scope of a witness's role includes certifying electronic records, testifying to the accuracy of the data, and explaining technical aspects if necessary. the witness can help establish the chain of custody and the reliability of electronic evidence. expert witness: an expert witness possesses specialized knowledge or skills in the field related to the electronic evidence. they may provide technical expertise and explain complex technical matters to the court. fact witness: a fact witness has firsthand knowledge of the events or circumstances related to the case and can testify about what they have observed or experienced. character witness: a character witness provides information about the character and reputation of a person involved in the case. this may not be directly related to electronic evidence but can be relevant in certain situations. collection of electronic evidence: during an investigation, electronic evidence is collected, and a chain of custody is maintained to preserve its integrity. certifying electronic records: a witness, often an expert, is required to certify the electronic records to establish their authenticity. testimony: witnesses may be called to testify in court. they are examined and cross-examined by the parties involved in the case. presentation of electronic evidence: witnesses can present electronic evidence, explain its relevance, and assist the court in understanding its implications. a witness may need to prepare a report that details the electronic evidence, its source, authenticity, and relevance to the case. the report should be clear and concise, and it should be presented in a format that the court can easily understand. the report may also include the witness's qualifications and a statement regarding the authenticity of the electronic evidence. honesty and truthfulness: a witness must provide accurate and truthful information to the court, without bias or prejudice. impartiality: a witness should remain impartial and not take sides in the case. professionalism: witnesses should conduct themselves professionally and respect the court's rules and procedures. confidentiality: witnesses should not disclose confidential or privileged information. cooperation: witnesses should cooperate with both the prosecution and defense during the legal process. compliance with the law: witnesses should comply with all legal obligations, including subpoena government, or agency responds to and manages cyberattacks and cyber terrorism incidents. it typically includes the following elements: incident identification and classification: the plan should define how different types of cyber incidents are classified, from minor security breaches to major cyber attacks. response teams: it outlines the roles and responsibilities of various response teams, such as it security teams, incident response teams, legal teams, and public relations teams.

communication protocols: clear communication is essential during a cyber crisis. the plan should specify how internal and external communications will be managed, including notifying affected parties, stakeholders, and the public. containment and mitigation: the plan should detail steps for containing the incident and mitigating its impact. this may include isolating affected systems, removing malware, and restoring services. legal and regulatory compliance: it should address legal and regulatory requirements related to data breaches and cyber incidents, ensuring that the organization remains compliant with applicable laws. recovery and restoration: the plan should outline the steps for recovering affected systems and restoring normal operations. post-incident analysis: after the incident is resolved, the plan should include a process for analyzing the incident, identifying lessons learned, and making improvements to prevent future attacks. training and exercises: regular training and exercises should be conducted to ensure that response teams are prepared to execute the plan effectively. cyber crisis management plans are crucial in today's digital landscape to identifying, preserving, analyzing and presenting digital evidence in a manner that is legally acceptable' (rodney mckemmish 19996 ). the study of evidence from attacks on computer systems in order to - a. gather all relevant information: collect all the evidence, witness statements, and investigative reports related to the case, including digital evidence. - b. clearly list the charges: specify the charges against the accused in a clear and concise manner. - c. include a statement of facts: present a detailed narrative of the incident, including the role of the accused, using specific information from the investigation. - d. list evidence and witnesses: enumerate the digital evidence you have seized and provide a list of witnesses who will testify in court. - e. ensure accuracy: verify all information for accuracy and consistency to avoid any legal challenges. - a. use write-blocking devices: when seizing digital media, use hardware or software write-blockers to prevent any data alterations during the acquisition process. - b. document the process: maintain a comprehensive chain of custody, including details of who handled the evidence, when, and under what conditions. - c. store evidence securely: keep digital evidence in a secure, tamperevident storage environment, with access limited to authorized personnel. - d. create backup copies: make forensic copies of digital evidence and work with those copies rather than the original media to avoid any accidental alterations. - e. record environmental conditions: document the conditions in which the digital media is stored, including temperature and humidity, to ensure the integrity of the evidence. - a. ensure admissibility: verify that the seized digital evidence complies with legal requirements for admissibility in court, such as chain of custody. - b. prepare expert witnesses: if required, ensure that your digital forensics experts are well-prepared to present and explain the digital evidence to the court. - c. organize and label evidence: present digital evidence in an organized and understandable manner, using clear labelling and indexes. - d. practice presenting evidence: practice presenting digital evidence in a clear and concise manner, ensuring that it supports your case effectively. - e. be prepared for cross-examination: anticipate potential questions and objections from the defense and prepare responses. - a. review the case thoroughly: reacquaint yourself with all aspects of the case, including witness statements, investigative reports, and the digital evidence. - b. prepare your witnesses: ensure that all witnesses are familiar with their testimonies and can present information accurately. - c. coordinate with experts: if experts will testify, review their findings, and discuss their testimony in advance. - d. familiarize yourself with court procedures: understand the court rules and procedures, including how evidence will be introduced and the order of examination. - e. anticipate objections: be ready to respond to objections from opposing counsel and prepare arguments to support the admissibility of your evidence. *****

- modus operandi: criminals can clone sim cards or use sim-less devices to gain unauthorized access to phone networks, intercept calls, and commit fraud or other illegal activities. - prevention: activate pin codes for your sim card and avoid sharing sensitive information over unsecured networks. regularly monitor your mobile account for unauthorized activities. - modus operandi: social engineering involves manipulating individuals into divulging confidential information, such as passwords or financial details. common methods include phishing, pretexting, and baiting. - prevention: be cautious about sharing personal information online and verify the identity of individuals or entities requesting sensitive data. educate yourself about common social engineering tactics. - modus operandi: criminals pose as customer service representatives to trick victims into sharing sensitive information or making fraudulent transactions. - prevention: verify the authenticity of customer service calls by contacting the company's official helpline. avoid sharing personal or financial information through unsolicited calls. - modus operandi: sending unsolicited messages or emails with fraudulent offers, phishing links, or malware attachments to deceive and steal from recipients. - prevention: use spam filters and avoid clicking on suspicious links or downloading attachments from unknown sources. never share personal or financial details through unsolicited messages. - modus operandi: fraudsters use deceptive tactics to manipulate victims into sharing upi credentials or otps, which are then used for unauthorized transactions. - prevention: be cautious about sharing otps or upi pins. verify the legitimacy of transactions and avoid sharing otps with anyone. - modus operandi: scammers post fake job listings, asking for upfront payments for job offers, stealing personal information, or engaging in other fraudulent activities. - prevention: research potential employers, and be sceptical of job offers that require upfront payments. use reputable job search websites and verify job listings. - modus operandi: criminals impersonate legitimate entities and request individuals to update their kyc (know your customer) details, leading to identity theft or fraud. - prevention: verify the legitimacy of kyc requests with the official organization. avoid sharing personal information via email or phone calls. - modus operandi: fraudulent sellers use online marketplaces like olx to scam buyers with fake listings, misrepresenting products or services. - prevention: conduct in-person meetings with the seller, verify the product's authenticity, and use secure payment methods. be cautious of deals that seem too good to be true. - modus operandi: scammers trick victims into purchasing gift cards or making gift payments under false pretenses, leading to financial losses. - prevention: only purchase gift cards from reputable sources. be cautious of requests for gift card payments from unknown individuals or entities. - modus operandi: hackers gain access to victims' whatsapp accounts and use them to send scam messages, gather personal information, or engage in illegal activities. - prevention: enable two-step verification on your whatsapp account, use strong passwords, and avoid sharing your verification codes with anyone. - modus operandi: scammers inform victims that they have won a lottery or prize and ask for a fee or personal details to claim the nonexistent reward.

- prevention: be skeptical of unsolicited lottery win notifications and never pay fees to claim a prize. verify the legitimacy of such offers with official lottery organizations. - modus operandi: online shopping scams involve fake websites, counterfeit products, and fraudulent transactions, resulting in financial losses for unsuspecting buyers. - prevention: shop from reputable websites with secure payment options. verify the legitimacy of the e-commerce platform and read reviews before making purchases. - modus operandi: criminals create malicious qr codes that, when scanned, lead to phishing sites, malware downloads, or unauthorized transactions. - prevention: only scan qr codes from trusted sources, and be cautious of unknown codes. regularly update your mobile device's operating system and security apps. - modus operandi: fraudulent online lenders exploit victims by promising loans and demanding fees upfront, resulting in financial loss and personal information theft. - prevention: verify the legitimacy of online lenders, avoid upfront fees, and read loan agreements carefully before accepting. - modus operandi: cybercriminals manipulate mobile service providers to swap a victim's sim card to gain access to their phone and accounts. - prevention: contact your mobile service provider to add extra security measures like pins or passwords to prevent unauthorized sim swaps. - modus operandi: criminals use techniques such as skimming, card trapping, or shoulder surfing to steal atm card details and withdraw funds. - prevention: inspect atms for skimming devices, shield your pin while entering it, and monitor your bank statements for unauthorized transactions. - modus operandi: perpetrators harass, threaten, or extort victims online, often using personal information or compromising images. - prevention: set strict privacy settings on your social media profiles and avoid sharing sensitive information or compromising images with strangers. - modus operandi: criminals impersonate individuals on social media to gather personal information or carry out fraudulent activities. - prevention: report and block impersonators on social media platforms. verify the authenticity of accounts before sharing personal information. - modus operandi: individuals or organizations stage fake accidents, injuries, or thefts to make fraudulent insurance claims. - prevention: be cautious of unsolicited insurance offers and verify the legitimacy of insurance providers. read policies carefully before purchasing. - modus operandi: scammers create deceptive ads to promote nonexistent products or services, tricking consumers into making payments. - prevention: avoid clicking on suspicious ads and verify the legitimacy of the products or services being advertised. - modus operandi: perpetrators promise high returns on investments, luring victims to invest in fraudulent schemes. - prevention: conduct due diligence on investment opportunities, seek advice from financial professionals, and be cautious of high-return promises. - modus operandi: criminals use spoofed caller ids to impersonate authorities or financial institutions, convincing victims to share sensitive information. - prevention: be skeptical of unsolicited calls and verify the identity of callers. avoid sharing personal or financial information over the phone.

- modus operandi: scammers pose as potential romantic partners on online dating or matrimonial platforms to deceive victims for financial gain. - prevention: use reputable dating or matrimonial websites, be cautious of romance scams, and avoid sharing sensitive information with online acquaintances. - open source intelligence (osint) is a multifaceted process of collecting, analyzing, and utilizing publicly available information from open sources to obtain intelligence and insights about a wide range of subjects, organizations, individuals, or events. osint is essential in various fields, including intelligence, law enforcement, cyber security, business, and academia. here's a more detailed explanation of osint: osint is distinct from classified or confidential sources of information. it relies exclusively on publicly available data and does not involve any unauthorized access, hacking, or espionage. open sources can encompass a broad spectrum of information, such as: websites: publicly accessible websites, including news sites, forums, blogs, and company websites. social media: information from platforms like facebook, twitter, linkedin, and instagram. public records: government records, court documents, property records, and financial reports that are accessible to the public. academic publications: research papers, theses, and other scholarly documents. news media: articles, reports, and broadcasts from reputable news outlets. publicly available databases: information in publicly accessible databases, directories, and repositories. osint practitioners use various methods and tools to collect data from these open sources. this may involve web scraping, using search engines, utilizing specialized osint software, monitoring social media, and accessing publicly available government or commercial databases. human intelligence, or "humint," is also a critical component, involving the collection of information through observation, interviews, or interactions with individuals. the collected data is then subjected to rigorous analysis. this can involve a range of techniques, including: correlation: finding connections between different pieces of information. pattern recognition: identifying trends or anomalies within the data. geospatial analysis: utilizing location data to map and analyze information. sentiment analysis: determining public sentiment or opinion on a given topic. link analysis: establishing relationships between individuals, organizations, or events. osint serves diverse purposes across various domains: national security: intelligence agencies use osint to assess potential threats, monitor geopolitical developments, and gather information about foreign entities. law enforcement: police and investigative agencies utilize osint to support criminal investigations, track suspects, and gather evidence. cyber security: osint helps identify vulnerabilities, detect cyber threats, and investigate cyber attacks. it's also used in threat intelligence to understand the tactics and motives of threat actors. business intelligence: companies employ osint for competitive analysis, market research, due diligence during mergers and acquisitions, and brand reputation monitoring. journalism: journalists use osint to fact-check, gather background information, and uncover stories by analyzing publicly available data and documents. osint practitioners must adhere to legal and ethical guidelines. this includes respecting individuals' privacy, not engaging in intrusive or illegal activities, and understanding the legal boundaries of information collection in various jurisdictions. osint experts use a wide range of tools and techniques, including search engines, social media analytics, data mining, open-source software, and specialized platforms like maltego, osint framework, and more. they may also employ advanced data analysis and visualization tools to make sense of large datasets. google: the most popular search engine for general information. bing: microsoft's search engine. duck packets for transmission. these packets are typically around 20-30 milliseconds in duration. packetization is necessary to efficiently transmit voice over data networks, as it allows data to be transmitted in smaller, manageable units. transmission: the voice packets are sent over the internet or an ip-based network. they are treated like any other data packets, such as those used for email destination. these network nodes help route the packets efficiently and can adapt to changes in network conditions to ensure a continuous and reliable connection. voice compression: to reduce the amount of data that needs to be transmitted, many voip systems use voice compression algorithms. these algorithms remove redundant or less important data from the voice signal before transmission, achieved through quality of service mechanisms in the network, which give voice packets higher priority to ensure low latency and minimal packet loss. voice decompression: upon reaching the crypto currencies and blockchain technology are closely related concepts, with cryptocurrencies being one of the most prominent applications of block chain technology. to understand them in detail, let's break down each of these concepts. blockchain technology is a decentralized and distributed ledger system that records transactions in a secure and transparent manner. it was originally created as the underlying technology for bitcoin, the first cryptocurrency, but it has since found applications in various fields beyond digital currencies. - a. decentralization: unlike traditional centralized systems (like banks), a block chain operates on a decentralized network of computers, also known as nodes. this means there is no central authority or intermediary controlling the system. - b. distributed ledger: the block chain is a ledger that records all transactions across the network. each transaction is grouped into a "block" and linked together in a chronological sequence, forming a "chain." - c. transparency: the entire transaction history is publicly available, and anyone can view the block chain to verify transactions. this transparency helps prevent fraud and corruption. - d. security: the block chain uses cryptographic techniques to secure transactions and control access. once a transaction is added to the block chain, it becomes very difficult to alter or delete it, ensuring the integrity of the data. - e. consensus mechanisms: block chain networks use consensus mechanisms (e.g., proof of work, proof of stake) to validate and confirm transactions. these mechanisms ensure that only legitimate transactions are added to the block chain. - f. smart contracts: some block chains support smart contracts, which are self-executing contracts with the terms of the agreement written directly into code. they automatically execute when predefined conditions are met. cryptocurrencies are digital or virtual currencies that use cryptography for security and operate on block chain technology. they serve as a medium of exchange, store of value, and unit of account, similar to traditional currencies like the us dollar or the euro. some of the most well-known cryptocurrencies include bitcoin, ethereum, and ripple (xrp). - a. digital and decentralized: cryptocurrencies exist only in digital form and rely on blockchain technology to function. they are not controlled by any central authority, such as a government or a central bank. - b. security: cryptocurrencies use cryptographic techniques to secure transactions and control the creation of new units. this makes them resistant to counterfeiting and fraud. - c. anonymity and privacy: while transactions are recorded on the blockchain and are publicly visible, users' identities are usually pseudonymous. this provides a level of privacy, but it's not entirely anonymous. - d. volatility: cryptocurrencies are known for their price volatility. their values can fluctuate significantly over short periods, which can be both an opportunity and a risk for investors. - e. use cases: cryptocurrencies can be used for various purposes, such as online purchases, investment, remittances, and as a means of transferring value across borders.

factors like user engagement with search results and click-through rates, as well as on-page seo elements. google's interface is minimalist and straightforward, with a single search bar and options for text and image searches. it also offers various services, such as google maps, google scholar, and google news, integrated into its search results page.

bing's interface is visually distinct, with a daily background image and a search bar. it provides quick answers and access to various microsoft services like outlook, onedrive, and office online. google's search results often include featured snippets, knowledge graphs, and information panels that provide concise answers to common queries. it also offers voice search and personalized search results based on your search history. bing has its own set of rich features, such as the "bing visual search" tool, which allows users to search the web using images. it also offers a rewards program called bing rewards, which lets users earn points for using bing and redeem them for various products and services. google is known for collecting extensive user data to personalize search results and serve targeted ads. users can manage their data privacy settings through their google accounts. bing also collects user data but generally has a more explicit focus on user privacy, offering options to opt out of personalized ads and data tracking. google has a significantly larger market share than bing and is the dominant search engine worldwide, with over 90% market share in some regions. bing, on the other hand, has a smaller market share but is still a significant player, particularly in the united states, where it holds around 7-10% of the search engine market share. carrot search is a search technology company that specializes in providing advanced search and text analysis solutions. the major differences between carrot search and traditional search engines like google and bing are: carrot search's primary focus is on clustering and visualizing search results. it organizes search results into groups or clusters based on their semantic similarity, which helps users explore related information effectively. traditional search engines like google and bing primarily provide a list of ranked search results based on their relevance to the query. carrot search uses advanced text analysis techniques to understand the concepts and relationships between words in documents. this allows users to explore search results based on the concepts or topics they are interested in. traditional search engines rely on keyword-based matching and ranking. carrot search offers faceted search capabilities, allowing users to filter and refine search results based on various attributes and facets, making it easier to find the most relevant information. traditional search engines provide filtering options, but carrot search's approach is more focused on facets and semantic attributes. image lookups refer to the process of searching for information or related images based on an uploaded or linked image. here are the major differences between various image lookup services: google images is a popular image search engine that allows users to search for images using keywords or by uploading an image. it uses algorithms to find visually similar images, and it can be used for various purposes, including finding information about objects in pictures or identifying similar images. bing visual search is a feature of the bing search engine that enables users to perform searches using images. it allows users to upload or capture images and search for related products, text, or similar images. it is often used for shopping and identifying objects in images. reverse image search services, such as tineye and saucenao, allow users to upload an image and find instances of that image on the web. these services are commonly used to identify the source of images, track down copyright violations, or find higher-resolution versions of an image. carrot search also offers a visual search component that can be used to search for related images based on an input image. carrot search's focus is on clustering and organizing similar images, making it useful for exploring collections of related visual content.

google: the world's most popular search engine. bing: microsoft's search engine with various features. duck duck go: a privacy-focused search engine. yahoo: offers search and various web services. carrot search: specializes in search and text analysis solutions. elastic search: a powerful open-source search and analytics engine. solr: an open-source search platform from the apache lucene project. gensim: a python library for topic modelling and document similarity analysis. stanford nlp: offers natural language processing tools and libraries. image lookup and visual search tools: google images: allows reverse image search and searching for images using keywords. bing visual search: microsoft's tool for searching by image or product. tin eye: a reverse image search engine that finds instances of an image on the web. sauce nao: a reverse image search tool for identifying anime and manga artwork. camfind: a mobile app for visual search using smart phone cameras. the dark web is a part of the internet that is intentionally hidden and not indexed by search engines. it is a small portion of the deep web, which is the part of the internet not accessible through standard search engines like google or bing. the dark web is intentionally hidden, and its content is not easily accessible to the average internet user. it is known for its anonymity and a range of activities, both legal and illegal, that take place there. two key aspects that contribute to the secrecy and anonymity of the dark web are the use of the tor network (the onion router) and the collection of information within this network. the tor network is a crucial tool for accessing the dark web. it is a system of volunteer-operated servers that allows people to browse the internet anonymously. tor directs internet traffic through a worldwide network of servers to conceal a user's location, making it difficult for anyone to trace their online activity back to them. it works by wrapping data packets in multiple layers of encryption, akin to layers of an onion, hence the name "the onion router." when a user accesses the tor network, their internet traffic is routed through a series of tor nodes, making it nearly impossible for anyone, including isps (internet service providers), government agencies, or hackers, to monitor their online activities or track their physical location.

the tor (the onion router) network provides users with a level of anonymity that makes it attractive to individuals who require privacy for legitimate reasons, such as whistle-blowers, activists, and people living under oppressive regimes. however, it also attracts users engaging in illicit activities due to the anonymity it offers. criminal activities on the dark web include the sale of drugs, firearms, stolen data, hacking tools, counterfeit currency, and various other illegal services. there are also forums and marketplaces where illegal goods and services are bought and sold. on the dark web, individuals and entities may collect information through various means. these could be for lawful purposes such as research or investigative journalism, or for illegal purposes like identity theft or blackmail. legal information collection: some researchers and journalists use the tor network to access and collect information from websites and forums that are not indexed by standard search engines. this may involve gathering data on specific subjects, tracking online communities, or documenting various aspects of the dark web for research purposes. illegal information collection: on the darker side of the dark web, there are entities that collect personal and sensitive information for nefarious purposes. this can include stealing and selling personal data, distributing malware, conducting phishing attacks, or engaging in cyber-espionage. the anonymous nature of the tor network makes it a breeding ground for such activities. note: osint techniques (covered in semester 1 book on computer and networks basics, cctns, it initiatives of telangana state police) - page 352 *****
